---
title: "Chapter 23: Analyzing and Synthesizing Information"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 23 of Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications."
book_id: "evaluation-theory-models-applications"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 23
part: "Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools"
source_pages: "593-624"
citation_key: "stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch23-chapter-23-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0001"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch23-chapter-23-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0002"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:principles-for-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0002"
  - "chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:analysis-of-qualitative-information:c0007"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2014</span><span>Part Four: Evaluation Tasks, Procedures, and Tools</span><span>Chapter 23</span><span>Source pages 593-624</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 23, "Analyzing and Synthesizing Information," from Daniel L. Stufflebeam and Chris L. S. Coryn's *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications* [@stufflebeam_coryn_2014_evaluation_theory_models_applications]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source chunks at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, review questions, checklists, exhibits, or extended passages. When a checklist or table is central, the note describes its function and leaves the source artifact in the evidence record.

## Core Argument

Analysis breaks evidence into interpretable findings; synthesis combines those findings with values to reach justified conclusions.

## Study Summary

Chapter 23 moves from information collection to analysis, synthesis, and judgment. The evaluator must answer descriptive, relational, and causal questions, but also combine evidence into bottom-line conclusions about value.

Quantitative analysis includes descriptive statistics, relational analysis, causal analysis, statistical significance, practical significance, confidence intervals, and effect sizes. The chapter emphasizes choosing methods whose assumptions fit the data and evaluation questions.

Qualitative analysis is treated as systematic discovery and interpretation. Evaluators define document sets, mark records, read samples, create grounded categories, code materials, summarize findings, and track evidence so claims can be defended.

Synthesis is the distinctive evaluative step. It combines findings across methods and sources, brings facts together with values, and resolves multiple value perspectives enough to support justified conclusions about merit, worth, significance, and probity.

For close reading, track how the chapter uses analysis, synthesis, quantitative analysis, and qualitative analysis while moving through rationale for conclusion-oriented evaluation, quantitative analysis, and qualitative analysis. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

## Key Concepts

- **analysis**: The process of organizing and interpreting evidence so it can answer evaluation questions.
- **synthesis**: The combination of findings, criteria, and value perspectives into an overall evaluative conclusion.
- **quantitative analysis**: Statistical or numerical analysis used to summarize, compare, estimate, or test evaluation evidence.
- **qualitative analysis**: Systematic interpretation of nonnumeric evidence such as interviews, observations, documents, and cases.
- **practical significance**: The real-world importance of a finding, apart from whether it is statistically significant.
- **effect size**: A quantitative estimate of the size of a difference or effect, useful for judging practical importance.
- **fact-value synthesis**: The evaluative act of combining empirical findings with value criteria to reach justified conclusions.
- **justified conclusions**: Conclusions whose logic from criteria, evidence, analysis, and limitations is explicit and defensible.

## Chapter Structure

- Rationale for conclusion-oriented evaluation.
- Quantitative analysis.
- Qualitative analysis.
- Synthesis.
- Justified conclusions.

## Practical Implications

- Choose analysis methods before collecting data when assumptions matter.
- Keep an audit trail from findings back to sources.
- Separate statistical significance from practical and evaluative significance.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What value problem does Chapter 23 help evaluators handle?"]
  Central["Concept: Analyzing and Synthesizing Information"]
  Concept1["Concept: analysis"]
  Concept2["Concept: synthesis"]
  Mechanism1["Mechanism: rationale for conclusion-oriented evaluation"]
  Mechanism2["Mechanism: quantitative analysis"]
  Implication1["Policy implication: Choose analysis methods before collecting data when assumptions matter."]
  Implication2["Policy implication: Keep an audit trail from findings back to sources."]
  Question["Open question: What evidence would make this guidance credible in a live evaluation?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef evidence fill:#fff2df,stroke:#b45309,color:#7c2d12;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information](chapter-22-collecting-evaluative-information.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings](chapter-24-communicating-evaluation-findings.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What value problem does Chapter 23 help an evaluator diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before applying this chapter in a live evaluation?
3. How would this chapter change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, reporting, or use?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## Source Chunks

<details class="source-chunks">
<summary>Source chunks</summary>
<ul class="source-chunk-list">
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch23-chapter-23-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0001</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:ch23-chapter-23-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0002</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:principles-for-analyzing-and-synthesizing-information:c0002</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:evaluation-theory-models-applications:analysis-of-qualitative-information:c0007</code></li>
</ul>
</details>

## References

- Stufflebeam, D. L., & Coryn, C. L. S. (2014). *Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications*. Jossey-Bass. Chapter 23, pages 593-624.
- Saved retrieval metadata: `data/research-library/evidence/evaluation-theory-models-applications/chapter-evidence-summary.json`.
